## Per-question answers

### `-7013890438520559398`
> The answer depends on which goal-counting methodology is used.

Pelé's 1281 goals are recognized by FIFA as the highest total achieved by a professional footballer. [alce::-7013890438520559398::p0]
> However, the Rec.Sport.Soccer Statistics Foundation (RSSSF) recognizes only 767 goals in official mode for Pelé, which places him third after Josef Bican (805 goals) and Romario (772 goals). [alce::-7013890438520559398::p0]
> According to RSSSF estimates, Josef Bican scored at least 805 goals in all competitive matches, which would make him the most prolific scorer of all time. [alce::-7013890438520559398::p2]

Cited: alce::-7013890438520559398::p0, alce::-7013890438520559398::p2

### `6962706727076805207`
> A bait car, also called a decoy car, hot car, or trap car, is used by law enforcement agencies to catch car thieves. [alce::6962706727076805207::p0]
> These vehicles are modified with audio/video surveillance technology and can be remotely monitored and controlled. [alce::6962706727076805207::p0]
> Bait cars set up to catch car thieves may include GPS tracking and a "kill switch" that allows police to remotely disable the engine and lock all doors. [alce::6962706727076805207::p0]

> The New Testament was written by early Christians, believed to be mostly Jewish disciples of Christ. [alce::1029755223764707835::p3]
> The books of the New Testament were all or nearly all written by Jewish Christians during the first and early second centuries. [alce::1029755223764707835::p2]
> Specific authors include Luke, who wrote the Gospel of Luke and the Book of Acts. [alce::1029755223764707835::p2]
> The Pauline epistles are thirteen books traditionally attributed to Paul of Tarsus. [alce::1029755223764707835::p1]
> Scholars have also traditionally attributed the Gospel of Mark to Mark the Evangelist, the Gospel of Matthew to Matthew the Apostle, and the Gospel of John to John the Apostle, though opinion is widely divided on these attributions and there is no widespread consensus. [alce::1029755223764707835::p4]

### `4965896127144803833`
> The downfall of the Inca Empire was primarily caused by epidemic diseases introduced by Spanish contact. [alce::4965896127144803833::p1]
> Smallpox spread rapidly through the Inca Empire beginning in Colombia, likely aided by the efficient Inca road system, even before Spanish conquistadors arrived in the region. [alce::4965896127144803833::p2]
> The Andean people had been isolated for millennia and therefore had no immunity to foreign diseases, unlike Europeans who had been exposed to smallpox for roughly two centuries. [alce::4965896127144803833::p0]
> The disease was particularly devastating: it killed Inca ruler Wayna Capac, which triggered a civil war in the Inca Empire that preceded the Spanish conquest efforts. [alce::4965896127144803833::p1]
> Multiple subsequent epidemics followed, including probable Typhus in 1546, influenza and smallpox together in 1558, smallpox again in 1589, diphtheria in 1614, and measles in 1618. [alce::4965896127144803833::p2]
> The population decline was catastrophic, falling from an estimated 9 to 16 million people before Spanish arrival to around 600,000 by 1620, mainly because of infectious diseases. [alce::4965896127144803833::p4]
> The population was so devastated by disease that they could hardly resist the foreign forces. [alce::4965896127144803833::p3]

Cited: alce::4965896127144803833::p0, alce::4965896127144803833::p1, alce::4965896127144803833::p2, alce::4965896127144803833::p3, alce::4965896127144803833::p4

### `-7403082197877166868`
> The United States left the gold standard in 1971. [alce::-7403082197877166868::p1]
> President Richard Nixon announced on August 15, 1971, that the United States would no longer convert dollars to gold at a fixed value, thus abandoning the gold standard for foreign exchange. [alce::-7403082197877166868::p1]
> This event became known as the Nixon Shock and marked the dollar's transition from the gold standard to a fiat currency. [alce::-7403082197877166868::p4]

Cited: alce::-7403082197877166868::p1, alce::-7403082197877166868::p4

### `-5001543574321430501`
> As blues moved into Chicago's South Side, Chicago blues developed. [alce::-5001543574321430501::p3]
> Chicago blues is an electric blues style of urban blues that evolved from classic blues following the Great Migration of African Americans from the southern United States to industrial cities of the north. [alce::-5001543574321430501::p3]
> This new Chicago blues sound was pioneered in the early 1950s by Delta bluesmen such as Muddy Waters, Howlin' Wolf and Little Walter, and was characterized by amplified instruments while harking back to a Delta-influenced sound. [alce::-5001543574321430501::p2]

Cited: alce::-5001543574321430501::p2, alce::-5001543574321430501::p3

> Modern conservatives in the 1980s wanted to reduce government regulation, taxes, and the size of government. [alce::-4465070934230250382::p2]
> Economic conservatives favored less government regulation and lower taxes. [alce::-4465070934230250382::p4]
> Reagan successfully lowered marginal tax rates, most notably for those at the top of the income distribution. [alce::-4465070934230250382::p1]
> Additionally, the Thatcher government cut the maximum capital gains taxes and privatized industries and public housing. [alce::-4465070934230250382::p3]
> The 1979 Conservative manifesto stated the intention to reduce inflation and taxation through substantial economies.

### `506938807337302957`
> FIFA (Fédération Internationale de Football Association) is an international governing body of association football, futsal, and beach soccer. [alce::506938807337302957::p0]
> It was founded in 1904 to oversee international competition among national associations. [alce::506938807337302957::p0]
> FIFA is responsible for organizing football's major international tournaments, notably the World Cup which commenced in 1930 and the Women's World Cup which commenced in 1991. [alce::506938807337302957::p0]
> FIFA has 211 member countries. [alce::506938807337302957::p0]
> These members are national associations, and each of the 211 members has one vote in the FIFA Congress, which is the supreme legislative body of FIFA. [alce::506938807337302957::p1]

Cited: alce::506938807337302957::p0, alce::506938807337302957::p1

> The cost to raise the Costa Concordia varied depending on which phase of the salvage operation is being referenced. The initial salvage operation was anticipated to cost $300 million. [alce::-119069581933917963::p3]

### `1719387094791600068`
> The muscle fibers under the control of a single motor axon are the skeletal muscle fibers that the motor neuron innervates. [alce::1719387094791600068::p1]
> A motor unit is made up of a motor neuron and the skeletal muscle fibers innervated by that motor neuron's axonal terminals. [alce::1719387094791600068::p2]
> All muscle fibers in a motor unit are of the same fiber type. [alce::1719387094791600068::p2]
> When a motor unit is activated, all of its fibers contract. [alce::1719387094791600068::p2]

### `8216960630679687356`
> Newspaper editor John O'Sullivan is generally credited with coining the term "manifest destiny" in 1845. [alce::8216960630679687356::p0]
> Manifest destiny was a widely held belief in the United States that its settlers were destined to expand across North America. [alce::8216960630679687356::p2]
> The term captured the idea that the young American nation was destined to rule all of the North American continent. [alce::8216960630679687356::p3]
> Manifest destiny was an expression of conviction in the morality and value of expansionism that complemented other popular ideas of the era, including American exceptionalism and Romantic nationalism. [alce::8216960630679687356::p1]

Cited: alce::8216960630679687356::p0, alce::8216960630679687356::p1, alce::8216960630679687356::p2, alce::8216960630679687356::p3

### `-9150237805953541495`
> Emory Bogardus tried to evaluate attitude through social distance. [alce::-9150237805953541495::p1]
> He created the Bogardus social distance scale, a psychological testing scale designed to empirically measure people's willingness to participate in social contacts of varying degrees of closeness with members of diverse social groups. [alce::-9150237805953541495::p3]
> For Bogardus, social distance functioned as a measure of how much or little sympathy the members of a group feel for another group. [alce::-9150237805953541495::p1]

Cited: alce::-9150237805953541495::p1, alce::-9150237805953541495::p3

### `-2777825999784077693`
> Karl Landsteiner, an Austrian physician, figured out a way to determine the type of blood. [alce::-2777825999784077693::p0]
> In 1900, he discovered that blood sera from different persons would clump together when mixed in test tubes. [alce::-2777825999784077693::p0]
> Based on his observations in 1901, he classified human blood into three groups. [alce::-2777825999784077693::p0]
> He identified these three blood groups as A, B, and O. [alce::-2777825999784077693::p3]

Cited: alce::-2777825999784077693::p0, alce::-2777825999784077693::p3

### `2469112227919120438`
> The creation of the Senate was based on different models depending on which Senate is being referenced:

For the Ceylon Senate, it was based on recommendations of the Soulbury Commission and was modelled on the House of Lords in the United Kingdom. [alce::2469112227919120438::p0]
> For the American Senate, it was intended to be an American version of the House of Lords, with John Dickinson stating it should bear "as strong a likeness to the British House of Lords as possible." [alce::2469112227919120438::p2]
> For the Australian Senate, it was modelled in part after the United States Senate, by giving equal representation to each state, rather than being modelled solely after the House of Lords as the Canadian Senate was. [alce::2469112227919120438::p3]

Cited: alce::2469112227919120438::p0, alce::2469112227919120438::p2, alce::2469112227919120438::p3

### `4537952778249650608`
> The polar regions experience the greatest annual change in daylight hours. [alce::4537952778249650608::p2]
> At the poles, there are 24 hours of summer daylight (the midnight sun) and complete darkness during winter, representing the maximum possible variation. [alce::4537952778249650608::p4]
> In contrast, at the Equator, the daytime period always lasts about 12 hours regardless of season, showing no annual change. [alce::4537952778249650608::p1]

### `2697046418313369999`
> The Mouth of Truth in Rome is a marble mask that stands against the left wall of the portico of the Santa Maria in Cosmedin church, at the Piazza della Bocca della Verità. [alce::2697046418313369999::p0]
> The massive marble mask weighs about 1300 kg and probably depicts the face of the sea god Oceanus. [alce::2697046418313369999::p0]
> The eyes, nostrils and mouth are open. [alce::2697046418313369999::p0]
> Historians are not quite certain about the original purpose of the disc, though it was possibly used as a drain cover in the nearby Temple of Hercules Victor. [alce::2697046418313369999::p2]
> It is also thought that cattle merchants used it to drain the blood of cattle sacrificed to the god Hercules. [alce::2697046418313369999::p2]
> A legend states that if a person places his hand inside the mouth and swears falsely, the mouth will close and sever the hand. [alce::2697046418313369999::p3]
> However, there is no attested case of such an event taking place. [alce::2697046418313369999::p3]

Cited: alce::2697046418313369999::p0, alce::2697046418313369999::p2, alce::2697046418313369999::p3

### `6630776554188306922`
> The smell of vanilla comes from the vanilla orchid fruit (seed pod). [alce::6630776554188306922::p0]
> Specifically, as the fruit dries on the plant, phenolic compounds crystallize, and the fruit then releases the distinctive vanilla smell. [alce::6630776554188306922::p0]
> The main aroma and flavor components of vanilla are vanillin and 4-hydroxybenzaldehyde. [alce::6630776554188306922::p4]

### `6945133188536552979`
> The idea of representative government first developed in the Roman Republic. [alce::6945133188536552979::p0]
> The Roman Republic was the first known government in the western world to have a representative government, with supreme power held by the people and their elected representatives. [alce::6945133188536552979::p0]
> However, the formalization of representative parliaments as a distinct institutional form occurred later in medieval Spain, when King Alfonso IX convened the first cortes in León in 1188 CE. [alce::6945133188536552979::p4]

### `6990604505432466959`
> Pax Romana is Latin for "Roman Peace" and refers to a long period of relative peace and stability experienced by the Roman Empire between the accession of Caesar Augustus and the death of Marcus Aurelius, lasting approximately 206 years from 27 BC to AD 180. [alce::6990604505432466959::p0]
> It is important because during this period, the Roman Empire achieved its greatest territorial extent and its population reached a maximum of up to 70 million. [alce::6990604505432466959::p0]
> Additionally, the concept of Pax Romana was highly influential, and attempts to imitate it occurred in the Byzantine Empire and in the Christian West, where it morphed into the Peace and Truce of God. [alce::6990604505432466959::p1]

Cited: alce::6990604505432466959::p0, alce::6990604505432466959::p1

### `-4508291966620018192`
> The highest temperatures on Earth are recorded in different locations depending on the measurement type.

According to the World Meteorological Organization (WMO), the highest registered air temperature on Earth was recorded at Furnace Creek Ranch, California, located in the Death Valley desert in the United States, on July 10, 1913. [alce::-4508291966620018192::p1]
> However, the highest recorded ground surface temperature was 93.9 °C (201 °F), recorded in Furnace Creek, Death Valley, California, United States on July 15, 1972. [alce::-4508291966620018192::p2]
> Additionally, satellite measurements have recorded even higher temperatures, with the highest recorded temperature taken by a satellite measured in the Flaming Mountains of China in 2008. [alce::-4508291966620018192::p3]

Cited: alce::-4508291966620018192::p1, alce::-4508291966620018192::p2, alce::-4508291966620018192::p3

### `-5824736776762973289`
> Yang (陽 or 阳) in Chinese has multiple meanings:

In its most basic geographic sense, "yang" refers to the "sunny side" of a mountain or the "north bank of a river." [alce::-5824736776762973289::p0]
> In Chinese philosophy, "yang" represents the "positive/active/male principle in nature." [alce::-5824736776762973289::p2]
> The character itself is etymologically connected to the sun, as the phonetic component "yáng" "bright" originally pictured the "sun" with "rays coming down." [alce::-5824736776762973289::p1]
> The term is semantically complex and can also refer to "the sun," "open; overt," "belonging to this world," and in linguistics, "masculine." [alce::-5824736776762973289::p2]

Cited: alce::-5824736776762973289::p0, alce::-5824736776762973289::p1, alce::-5824736776762973289::p2

### `-5942352520455511076`
> Adipose tissue can be found in several locations throughout the body. It is located beneath the skin as subcutaneous fat. [alce::-5942352520455511076::p4]
> It accumulates around internal organs as visceral fat, which is packed between organs in the abdominal cavity. [alce::-5942352520455511076::p3]
> Adipose tissue is also found in bone marrow as yellow bone marrow. [alce::-5942352520455511076::p4]
> It can be found intermuscularly, interspersed within skeletal muscle. [alce::-5942352520455511076::p4]
> Additionally, adipose tissue is present in breast tissue. [alce::-5942352520455511076::p4]
> In the integumentary system, it accumulates in the subcutaneous layer of the skin, providing insulation. [alce::-5942352520455511076::p1]

> The German invasion army had entered Paris on 14 June 1940, following the Battle of France. [alce::-596092551637359468::p3]

### `4653868131958586810`
> NAT stands for Network Address Translation, which is a method of remapping one IP address space into another by modifying network address information in the IP header of packets while they are in transit across a traffic routing device. [alce::4653868131958586810::p0]
> The technique was originally used as a shortcut to avoid the need to readdress every host when a network was moved. [alce::4653868131958586810::p0]
> It has become a popular and essential tool in conserving global address space in the face of IPv4 address exhaustion. [alce::4653868131958586810::p0]
> NAT works by hiding an entire IP address space, usually consisting of private IP addresses, behind a single IP address in another, usually public address space. [alce::4653868131958586810::p2]
> One Internet-routable IP address of a NAT gateway can be used for an entire private network. [alce::4653868131958586810::p0]

Cited: alce::4653868131958586810::p0, alce::4653868131958586810::p2

### `2900239413928677063`
> The hottest reliably recorded air temperature on Earth is 134 °F (56.7 °C), which was recorded at Furnace Creek, California on July 10, 1913. [alce::2900239413928677063::p0]
> However, there is a higher temperature that was previously recognized: 58 °C was recorded at Al-Aziziya, Libya, though scientists argue this temperature is inaccurate as the thermometer was positioned above the tarmac floor and in the direction of the sun. [alce::2900239413928677063::p4]
> If measuring ground surface temperature rather than air temperature, the highest recorded natural ground surface temperature on Earth is 201 °F (93.9 °C), recorded at Furnace Creek on July 15, 1972. [alce::2900239413928677063::p0]

Cited: alce::2900239413928677063::p0, alce::2900239413928677063::p4

### `-604099956838475859`
> Benjamin is a popular given name for males derived from Hebrew "Binyāmīn," translating as "Son of my right
> ." [alce::-604099956838475859::p0]
> The name originated from the biblical son of Jacob, who was the last-born of Jacob's thirteen children and the second and last son of Rachel in Jewish, Christian and Islamic tradition. [alce::-604099956838475859::p2]
> According to the Hebrew Bible, Benjamin's name arose when Jacob deliberately changed the original name "Benoni" (meaning "son of my pain") to Benjamin, since Benoni was an allusion to Rachel's death immediately after giving birth. [alce::-604099956838475859::p3]

Cited: alce::-604099956838475859::p0, alce::-604099956838475859::p2, alce::-604099956838475859::p3

### `-7338374818994111335`
> Diffusion occurs in the lungs and skin as part of the excretory system. The lungs diffuse gaseous wastes, such as carbon dioxide, from the bloodstream as a normal part of respiration. [alce::-7338374818994111335::p3]
> In amphibians, waste products are expelled by diffusion through the skin. [alce::-7338374818994111335::p2]

Cited: alce::-7338374818994111335::p2, alce::-7338374818994111335::p3

### `4991056088698404341`
> The TV show The Crossing was filmed in British Columbia, Canada. [alce::4991056088698404341::p2]

> The beach where the refugees arrive is near Ucluelet on Vancouver Island, while the sheriff's office and some other locations were filmed in and around the village of Britannia Beach, north of Vancouver. [alce::4991056088698404341::p0]
> The Oceanic Plaza in Vancouver was the setting for the Seattle offices, and other scenes were filmed in and around the city. [alce::4991056088698404341::p0]

### `2188695242961419104`
> In the present format of the UEFA Champions League, 32 teams play in the group stage. [alce::2188695242961419104::p0]
> These 32 teams are drawn into eight groups of four teams each and play each other in a double round-robin system. [alce::2188695242961419104::p0]
> However, the total number of teams that participate in the competition from the qualifying rounds onwards is much larger. A total of 79, 80, or 81 teams from UEFA member associations are expected to participate in the 2019–20 UEFA Champions League. [alce::2188695242961419104::p2]

### `-285728796551513770`
> The Roman Catholic and Eastern Orthodox split due to a combination of theological, political, and cultural factors. [alce::-285728796551513770::p0]
> The Schism was the culmination of theological and political differences between the Christian East and West which had developed over the preceding centuries. [alce::-285728796551513770::p0]
> Prominent theological issues included the procession of the Holy Spirit and the filioque clause, as well as disputes over papal authority. [alce::-285728796551513770::p1]
> These doctrinal disputes were exacerbated by cultural and linguistic differences between Latins and Greeks. [alce::-285728796551513770::p1]
> The Orthodox East perceived the Papacy as taking on monarchical characteristics that were not acceptable to them. [alce::-285728796551513770::p1]
> The formal break occurred in 1054, when the papacy and the patriarchy of Constantinople clashed over papal supremacy and excommunicated each other. [alce::-285728796551513770::p2]
> This led to the division of Christianity into two Churches—the Western branch became the Roman Catholic Church and the Eastern branch the Eastern Orthodox Church. [alce::-285728796551513770::p2]

Cited: alce::-285728796551513770::p0, alce::-285728796551513770::p1, alce::-285728796551513770::p2

### `-8080892130396390822`
> The saying "man's best friend" has multiple documented origins. The first recorded use of a related phrase was by Frederick the Great of Prussia, who referred to one of his Italian Greyhounds as his best friend. [alce::-8080892130396390822::p1]
> Earlier, Voltaire had written about this concept in his "Dictionnaire philosophique" of 1764. [alce::-8080892130396390822::p2]
> In the United States, the earliest citation is traced to a poem printed in "The New-York Literary Journal, Volume 4" in 1821. [alce::-8080892130396390822::p1]
> The phrase was likely popularized by its use in a poem by Ogden Nash. [alce::-8080892130396390822::p0]
> The saying has since become a common colloquialism referring to dogs' millennia-long history of close relations, loyalty, and companionship with humans. [alce::-8080892130396390822::p0]

Cited: alce::-8080892130396390822::p0, alce::-8080892130396390822::p1, alce::-8080892130396390822::p2

### `-3840313627795945784`
> The answer depends on which country's federal law you're asking about, as the sources describe multiple nations:

**Canada:** R.B. Bennett's government passed the "Employment and Social Insurance Act" in 1935. [alce::-3840313627795945784::p0]
> However, this was ruled unconstitutional, and the first Canadian unemployment insurance system was adopted in 1940. [alce::-3840313627795945784::p3]
> **United States:** President Roosevelt signed an Act on August 14, 1935, which provided benefits to the unemployed (along with retirees). [alce::-3840313627795945784::p2]
> **United Kingdom:** The Unemployment Insurance Act 1920 expanded provisions for unemployment benefits. [alce::-3840313627795945784::p1]

### `-3529909367855134284`
> The two types of crust that make up the surface of the Earth are continental crust and oceanic crust. [alce::-3529909367855134284::p2]
> Continental crust is thicker, averaging about 35 kilometers thick under the continents, and is composed of less dense felsic rocks like granite. [alce::-3529909367855134284::p3]
> Oceanic crust is thinner, averaging only 7–10 kilometers beneath the oceans, and is composed of denser mafic rocks like basalt. [alce::-3529909367855134284::p1]

### `3880667713687536806`
> The sources do not identify a single "first person who discovered electricity." However, they document the earliest recorded observations and scientific contributions:

Thales of Miletus, writing around 600 BC, noted that rubbing fur on substances such as amber would cause them to attract specks of dust and other light objects. [alce::3880667713687536806::p4]
> The ancient Greeks noticed that amber attracted small objects when rubbed with fur, which is one of humanity's earliest recorded experiences with electricity. [alce::3880667713687536806::p0]
> William Gilbert made the first systematic scientific study of electricity in 1600 when he wrote "De Magnete," in which he distinguished static electricity from magnetism and coined the term "electricus." [alce::3880667713687536806::p3]

Cited: alce::3880667713687536806::p0, alce::3880667713687536806::p3, alce::3880667713687536806::p4

> Socrates used a question and answer method of arriving at important truths. [alce::-4320314591805585520::p0]

### `-8300695731604896849`
> The first alternator to produce alternating current was constructed by French instrument maker Hippolyte Pixii in 1832. [alce::-8300695731604896849::p1]
> This device was based on Michael Faraday's principles and consisted of a revolving horseshoe magnet passing over two wound-wire coils. [alce::-8300695731604896849::p2]
> However, the development of the first practical alternating current electric system came later. Sebastian Ziani de Ferranti built the world's first practical transformer-based alternating current power system at Great Barrington, Massachusetts in 1886. [alce::-8300695731604896849::p3]

### `-7301225247880986147`
> The Great Wall of China had multiple phases of construction beginning at different times. The earliest recorded wall-building effort was commanded by King Xuan of the Western Zhou dynasty (r. 827–782 BCE), who ordered General Nan Zhong to build a wall in the northern regions. [alce::-7301225247880986147::p3]
> During the Warring States period, various states including Qin, Wei, Zhao, Qi, Yan, and Zhongshan constructed extensive fortifications, with the state of Qi beginning construction of the Great Wall of Qi in 685 BCE. [alce::-7301225247880986147::p0]
> The State of Wei built walls in 361 BC and 356 BC. [alce::-7301225247880986147::p2]
> The Great Wall as it is more commonly known was built starting in the Qin dynasty (221–207 BC), when Qin Shi Huang unified China and connected the walls of the northern states Yan, Zhao, and Qin. [alce::-7301225247880986147::p1]
> However, its present form was mostly constructed during the Ming dynasty (1368–1644 AD). [alce::-7301225247880986147::p1]

Cited: alce::-7301225247880986147::p0, alce::-7301225247880986147::p1, alce::-7301225247880986147::p2, alce::-7301225247880986147::p3

### `305726888559328341`
> Metabolic activity in a cell occurs in multiple locations depending on the specific pathway. Most cellular metabolic activities take place within the cytoplasm. [alce::305726888559328341::p0]
> The majority of metabolic processes and metabolites occur in the cytosol, which is the liquid found inside cells. [alce::305726888559328341::p1]
> Major metabolic pathways that occur in the cytosol include glycolysis, the pentose phosphate pathway, and fatty acid biosynthesis. [alce::305726888559328341::p2]
> However, some metabolic pathways occur in other cellular compartments; for instance, the citric acid cycle, electron transport chain, and oxidative phosphorylation all take place in the mitochondrial membrane. [alce::305726888559328341::p2]

> The term "cupboard love" is a British idiom used most commonly in the United Kingdom and Australia. [alce::7803488668042298223::p2]
> It describes the selfish, greedy or insincere affection displayed towards another person in order to get what they want. [alce::7803488668042298223::p2]
> The term originates from a psychological learning theory of the 1950s and 1960s based on research by Sigmund Freud, Anna Freud, Melanie Klein and Mary Ainsworth, which theorized that infants develop attachment primarily through the satisfaction of their physiological needs, particularly food. [alce::7803488668042298223::p1]
> In this theory, an infant demonstrates characteristics perceived as affection and love towards the mother in order to be fed. [alce::7803488668042298223::p2]

Cited: alce::7803488668042298223::p1, alce::7803488668042298223::p2

### `5278020544239312824`
> The phrase "the pen is mightier than the sword" was coined by English author Edward Bulwer-Lytton in 1839 for his play "Richelieu; Or the Conspiracy". [alce::5278020544239312824::p0]
> However, the idea behind this phrase had earlier predecessors. The Assyrian sage Ahiqar, who reputedly lived during the early 7th century BC, coined the first known version of this phrase, with one copy of the Teachings of Ahiqar dating to about 500 BC stating, "The word is mightier than the sword." [alce::5278020544239312824::p2]

Cited: alce::5278020544239312824::p0, alce::5278020544239312824::p2

### `3055530995120092075`
> The Navigation Acts directed the flow of goods between England and the colonies. [alce::3055530995120092075::p1]
> These laws were a long series of English statutes that regulated English ships, shipping, trade, and commerce between England and its colonies, and restricted foreigners' participation in colonial trade. [alce::3055530995120092075::p1]
> Under the Navigation Acts, colonial goods could be carried only on English and colonial ships. [alce::3055530995120092075::p2]
> The colonies were expected to export raw materials such as wood, naval stores, tobacco, and rice, but not manufactured goods or finished products like furniture and ships, which Britain reserved for itself. [alce::3055530995120092075::p2]
> The system was first enacted in 1651 and was reenacted and broadened by the Act of 1660, with further development through the Navigation Acts of 1663, 1673, and 1696. [alce::3055530995120092075::p1]

Cited: alce::3055530995120092075::p1, alce::3055530995120092075::p2

> Based on the provided sources, there are conflicting historical claims about the longest and most expensive trial in US history:

> The oath sworn by members of the estates in 1789 on a tennis court was known as the Tennis Court Oath. [alce::7133553793803549181::p3]
> On 20 June 1789, the members of the French Third Estate took this oath, vowing "not to separate, and to reassemble wherever circumstances require, until the constitution of the kingdom is established." [alce::7133553793803549181::p3]
> Members of the Second and First estates also joined them in taking this oath. [alce::7133553793803549181::p4]

Cited: alce::7133553793803549181::p3, alce::7133553793803549181::p4

### `-6301642079833452476`
> On the standard Jeopardy! game board, contestants get different amounts of time depending on the difficulty level of the questions. For the first set of questions, contestants are allotted 30 seconds to respond. [alce::-6301642079833452476::p0]
> For the next set of questions, contestants are allotted 60 seconds to give their "final answer." [alce::-6301642079833452476::p0]
> For the final set of questions, the questions are un-timed. [alce::-6301642079833452476::p0]
> In Final Jeopardy!, contestants have 30 seconds to write their responses. [alce::-6301642079833452476::p2]

### `-5980018027276110696`
> The traditional view is that the Gospel of Luke and Acts were written by Luke, a physician and companion of Paul. [alce::-5980018027276110696::p1]
> However, the earliest manuscripts are anonymous, and modern scholars have challenged this traditional attribution. [alce::-5980018027276110696::p0]
> There is substantial evidence that both the Gospel of Luke and the Book of Acts share the same author, referred to as Luke–Acts. [alce::-5980018027276110696::p0]
> The majority of modern scholars reject the identification of the author as Luke the Evangelist due to contradictions between Acts and the authentic Pauline letters. [alce::-5980018027276110696::p2]
> Instead, many scholars believe Luke–Acts was written by an anonymous Christian author who may not have been an eyewitness to the events recorded in the text. [alce::-5980018027276110696::p3]

Cited: alce::-5980018027276110696::p0, alce::-5980018027276110696::p1, alce::-5980018027276110696::p2, alce::-5980018027276110696::p3

### `8664313149431483137`
> The musculoskeletal system is an organ system that gives humans the ability to move using their muscular and skeletal systems. [alce::8664313149431483137::p0]
> It is made up of the bones of the skeleton, muscles, cartilage, tendons, ligaments, joints, and other connective tissue that supports and binds tissues and organs together. [alce::8664313149431483137::p0]
> The musculoskeletal system's primary functions include supporting the body, allowing motion, and protecting vital organs. [alce::8664313149431483137::p0]
> The musculoskeletal system provides form, support, stability, and movement to the body. [alce::8664313149431483137::p0]
> Together with the skeletal system, the muscular system forms the musculoskeletal system, which is responsible for movement of the human body. [alce::8664313149431483137::p1]
> Muscles provide strength, balance, posture, movement and heat for the body. [alce::8664313149431483137::p2]

Cited: alce::8664313149431483137::p0, alce::8664313149431483137::p1, alce::8664313149431483137::p2

### `1986535647972136716`
> Sperm are made in the testes, specifically within the seminiferous tubules. [alce::1986535647972136716::p0]
> The spermatogonial stem cells adjacent to the inner tubule wall divide to produce immature sperm. [alce::1986535647972136716::p0]
> After initial production in the testes, the developing sperm travel through the seminiferous tubules and progress to the epididymis where they mature. [alce::1986535647972136716::p2]
> The testes are located in the scrotum, which is important because spermatogenesis requires a temperature 1°-8°C lower than normal body temperature. [alce::1986535647972136716::p0]

Cited: alce::1986535647972136716::p0, alce::1986535647972136716::p2

### `5059862462835087163`
> The five major circles of latitude on Earth are the Equator, the Arctic Circle, the Antarctic Circle, the Tropic of Cancer, and the Tropic of Capricorn. [alce::5059862462835087163::p1]
> The Equator is located at 0° latitude and is the only one of these five circles that is also a great circle. [alce::5059862462835087163::p1]
> The Tropic of Cancer and Tropic of Capricorn mark the northernmost and southernmost latitudes at which the sun may be seen directly overhead. [alce::5059862462835087163::p2]
> The Arctic Circle and Antarctic Circle are the polar circles, located at latitudes determined by Earth's axial tilt. [alce::5059862462835087163::p1]

### `5398357621914791303`
> The practice of baptism has multiple origins. Water purification rituals through immersion existed in many cultures before Christianity. [alce::5398357621914791303::p4]
> Various forms of baptism were practiced throughout the Jewish world, with the Greek noun "baptmos" being used to refer to ritual washing in Hellenistic Judaism. [alce::5398357621914791303::p1]
> John the Baptist adopted baptism as the central sacrament in his messianic movement, and he is considered a forerunner to Christianity. [alce::5398357621914791303::p2]
> Christians consider Jesus to have instituted the sacrament of baptism, with the Christian ritual tracing back to the baptism of Jesus by John the Baptist in the Jordan River. [alce::5398357621914791303::p4]

Cited: alce::5398357621914791303::p1, alce::5398357621914791303::p2, alce::5398357621914791303::p4
